item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's something interesting in here but it's deeply muddled by the multiple contradictory theses regarding what's going on (and evidence presented ultimately refutes any of the three, I think, so I'm assuming it's something altogether different). However, that interest was sufficient to keep me reading for the entirety of the (somewhat padded) length of it and the atmosphere it creates is really creepy, so +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A good skip that could have been told in less word counts. I feel that the Personal Account Excerpts of Dr. Mike O'Neill is too long-winded. I have to agree that the article flows smoothly from the containment procedure to the progression of addenda, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This unfolds so well. The pacing seems messy but it's perfect. I found myself initially thinking this was kinda silly or unserious (as unreliable-narrative SCPs can be) but it started to spook me out as I read it. The little hiccups in tone fit the disjointed alarm and self awareness of poor Dr. O'Neil and really draw the reader in. Well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it, actually. I admit that it did take me a second, and I got the first inklings of an idea of what might be going on, and you just built on it over time.
My take away was this, and I think the writer was trying to clue us in, as well; the wire was falling into a "void," as they put it. The lack of existence is what lies on the other side of the door. It seems as though anything that passes through is erased from the memories of everyone left alive. All of the redundant documents and repeated requests have been fulfilled and carried out, but every test completed would wipe your memory of completing the test.
The only pill I find hard to swallow is the idea that the Foundation wouldn't have caught on, though. I doubt they'd let things go this badly for such an extended period of time before realizing that something wasn't quite right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wish I felt intrigue rather than confusion. I like a good "unexplained mysteries" vibe, I would spend hours some nights looking at some of the most mysterious unsolved cases the world has to offer back in the day. This is clearly building off of that foundation, and it starts off so promising.

But it never capitalizes on it. It never gives that thing that I can sink my teeth into, that one aspect that weaves the bits into that captivating net that catches my intrigue. We get these journals that are supposed to tell a story but end up too disjointed to truly follow, and even if we could they might be utterly meaningless. Part of it might be that the researchers never really connect, I didn't really get invested in them, certainly not the writer. It seems clear that the video footage is supposed to be the payoff, but I don't really get anything from it, either because the build-up didn't clearly lead towards it or because again we're lead to feel like it's potentially unreliable. The whole "maybe it didn't happen at all" thing sort of kills the intrigue, because then we don't even really know what the story is.

It is a shame, because the fuel is there. This has the potential to reach that "unsolved mysteries" vibe. It just isn't there right now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Strange things operating off a twisted and haunting level of dream logic hit a soft spot for me. It does not quite reach the level of unease of SCP-2470 and it lacks the emotional punch that one has, but it was an entertaining read regardless. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You've still got an "SPC" in there, and this has nothing to do with punching sharks. There's a good hook in the idea that there's no record of the door being opened, despite attempts to do so. Like, there are plenty of creepy doors on this site, but that's better than just "we can't open it". But I don't think you quite approach that idea from the right angle. If nothing else, the journal entries get goofy toward the end. I can at least remove my downvote, though.
